title: 5 Dangerous Foods
full_text: Dangerous Foods: 5 Global Delicacies That Can Kill You - Palate Asia Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Main Eat Drink Reviews Insider At Home About Us Download Our Magazine Q2 2026 Q1 2026 Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 Now Reading: Dangerous Foods: 5 Global Delicacies That Can Kill You 1 01 Dangerous Foods: 5 Global Delicacies That Can Kill You Main // Eat // Drink // Reviews // Insider // At Home // About Us // Download Our Magazine // Q2 2026 // Q1 2026 // Vol 1 Palate Dining Guide 2025-2026 // Home Eat Dangerous Foods: 5 Global Delicacies That Can Kill You Dangerous Foods: 5 Global Delicacies That Can Kill You Colin Gomez Eat 3 days ago Share Not every dangerous food is the result of poor judgement. Sometimes it’s the result of generations of people looking at something poisonous, toxic, or otherwise inedible and deciding to figure it out anyway. Of all the food safety advice out there, perhaps the most fundamental is: if it can kill you, don’t eat it. Sounds sensible enough, doesn’t it? And not exactly difficult to adhere to either. Yet humanity has spent millennia treating that maxim as more of a suggestion than a rule. Nature is not short on poisonous plants, toxic animals, and all manner of ingredients that seem determined not to become food under any circumstances. But did we say, “okay, fair enough,” and just leave them alone? No. That doesn’t much sound like us. We’ve somehow managed to weaponise the very particles that make up matter itself, remember? Our response to nature’s quite reasonable insistence on not being eaten is to take things and subject them to all sorts of culinary experimentation in an effort to quash that resistance. Whether it’s boiling, fermenting, hanging them out to dry, or just straight up burying them, we’ve gone to great lengths indeed to pummel nature into submission for even having the audacity to want to live. And you know what? We’ve largely succeeded in this regard. Here is a collection of dishes that, to an outsider, can seem completely baffling, but to the people who eat them, are beloved fixtures of their local cuisine. Photography: Brunk-Tan | Wikimedia Commons Pangium Edule (Buah Keluak) – Southeast Asia Starting a little closer to home, we’ve got our own entry in the “who thought this was a good idea?” list. You might think this sort of thing only happens elsewhere—in some far-off, vaguely “exotic” culinary tradition. I regret to inform you that questionable judgement is, in fact, universal. The seeds of the Pangium edule tree, more commonly known as buah keluak , contain hydrogen cyanide. In their raw state, they’re decidedly not something you’d want to snack on. Fortunately, generations before us figured out that if you boil the seeds, bury them in ash and soil, and then leave them to ferment for weeks on end, something rather remarkable happens. The once-toxic seeds emerge black, earthy, and intensely savoury. They’re perhaps best known for their role in Peranakan cooking, where they lend their distinctive flavour to dishes such as ayam buah keluak . Across the region, they also appear in stews and soups, adding a certain kind of richness you’d be hard-pressed to find in anything else. There’s something admirable, in a way, about the persistence involved. Here you have a poisonous seed and instead of leaving it at that, somebody said, “how about we bury it for a month and see what happens?” Photography: Kirk K | Flickr Sannakji – South Korea Sannakji is a Korean dish made from small octopus that is chopped and served immediately. It’s not poisonous, unlike most of the entries on this list, but rather its danger comes from its vigorous propensity to resist ingestion. The octopus isn’t alive anymore, so that’s a plus at least, but its nervous system remains active for a short time afterwards, causing its tentacles to continue writhing across the plate. It’s an arresting (read: unnerving) sight for sure, but the real risk comes from the suction cups. They can remain functional long enough to attach themselves to the inside of the mouth or throat, creating a genuine choking hazard if eaten carelessly. So if you ever decide you have the stomach for it, just make sure you have a healthy helping of patience to go with it — the kind required for very thorough chewing. Photography: Chris Wronski | Flickr Hákarl – Iceland Normally, you’d like your seafood to be as fresh as it possibly can. With the Greenland shark, however, it’s quite the opposite. You see, its flesh contains compounds that render it toxic when fresh, owing largely to the fact that the animal lacks a urinary system and, as a result, stores waste products directly in its tissues. For most people, this would probably be where the conversation ends. The Icelanders, however, had other ideas.
